title: Correlation Between Mood and Sleep
subtitle: When you sleep well, your mood improves by 18%

content.text:
Sleep-Mood Connection

Analysis shows a strong positive correlation: better sleep leads to better mood the next day.
content.headline: The Sleep-Mood Link
content.body: Your data reveals a powerful truth: when you get 7-8 hours of quality sleep, your mood score increases by 18% the following day. This is one of the strongest correlations in your data.
content.mantra: Sleep is medicine for your mood

content.text:
Statistical Evidence

Days after 7+ hours sleep: Mood avg 78/100
Days after 6-7 hours sleep: Mood avg 68/100
Days after <6 hours sleep: Mood avg 52/100
content.headline: The Data Speaks
content.body: Your historical data is clear: 7+ hours of sleep → 78/100 mood average. 6-7 hours → 68/100. Less than 6 hours → 52/100. Each additional hour of sleep adds roughly 10 points to your mood score.

content.text:
Sleep Quality Factors

Bedtime consistency: +8% mood impact
Dark room: +5% mood impact
No screens 30 min before bed: +4% mood impact
content.headline: What Improves Sleep Quality
content.body: To maximize this correlation: maintain consistent bedtime (8% boost), ensure a dark sleeping environment (5% boost), and eliminate screens 30 minutes before sleep (4% boost). Combined, these triple your sleep quality benefits.

content.text:
Action: Optimize Your Sleep

1. Set consistent bedtime: 10:30 PM
2. Prepare dark, cool room (65-68°F)
3. No screens after 10:00 PM
4. Track mood improvement (expect +18% this month)
content.headline: Your Sleep-Mood Optimization Plan
content.body: Implement these three changes: consistent 10:30 PM bedtime, dark/cool bedroom at 65-68°F, and phone/screens away by 10:00 PM. Track your mood for 30 days and expect to see approximately 18% improvement.
